edits.original_text: The world he was about to change didn't know his name yet. Rome did.
edits.revised_text: Marcus had catalogued three thousand faces in his census rounds across the eastern provinces. None had ever looked back at him like they were reading his thoughts.
edits.rationale: Creates immediate hook with Marcus in scene rather than vague thematic commentary

edits.original_text: Galilee's winter dampness crawled under his cloak and into his bones
edits.revised_text: Galilee's winter dampness seeped through his cloak, settling in his bones
edits.rationale: Replaces weak, clichéd metaphor with more precise language

edits.original_text: the kind of cold that made his left knee—broken in Thrace, badly healed—throb with each shift of weight
edits.revised_text: cold that made his left knee—broken in Germania, badly healed—throb with each shift of weight
edits.rationale: Removes weak qualifier and corrects chronological anachronism
edits.source_note: Line Editor - padding removal and Scientific Accuracy Editor - Thrace chronology

edits.original_text: Herod Antipas's administration ran its own census for tribute purposes, but Rome insisted on attaching military observers, officially to verify compliance, unofficially to identify threats
edits.revised_text: The Roman census ordered by Governor Pontius Pilate required local cooperation, with Herodian officials facilitating registration while Roman observers ensured compliance
edits.rationale: Corrects historical anachronism about census system
edits.source_note: Historical Authenticity Editor - anachronism taxation system

edits.original_text: Marcus drew the assignment because he spoke passable Aramaic
edits.revised_text: Marcus received orders for this duty because he spoke passable Aramaic
edits.source_note: Historical Authenticity Editor - military terminology anachronism

edits.original_text: "Name," Marcus said in Aramaic. His accent was terrible, but functional.
edits.revised_text: "Name." His Aramaic accent was terrible, but functional.
edits.rationale: Removes unnecessary dialogue tag when speaker is clear

edits.original_text: Marcus's scribe, Lucius, scratched duplicates onto wax tablets
edits.revised_text: Marcus's scribe, Lucius, made temporary notes on wax tablets before copying the official records onto papyrus
edits.rationale: Corrects anachronism about official record-keeping materials
edits.source_note: Historical Authenticity Editor - writing materials issue

edits.original_text: The census data would be copied to parchment later, sent to Caesarea
edits.revised_text: Later, scribes would copy the census data to parchment and send it to Caesarea
edits.rationale: Converts passive voice to active for stronger impact

edits.original_text: His hand moved to the stylus and stopped. The way a dog's ears flatten before thunder arrives.
edits.revised_text: His hand moved to the stylus and stopped. Something had changed—the way a dog's ears flatten before thunder arrives.
edits.rationale: Adds transitional sentence to signal shift in Marcus's awareness

edits.original_text: They weren't threatening. They were *reading* him.
edits.revised_text: They weren't threatening. They were cataloguing details—the way Marcus read military terrain before an assault.
edits.rationale: Shows rather than tells the 'reading' quality through specific comparison
edits.source_note: Immersive Experience Editor - showing vs telling

edits.original_text: Remaining unmarried this long suggested either serious poverty, social ostracism, or deliberate choice.
edits.revised_text: Remaining unmarried at thirty-two violated fundamental Jewish social expectations—only the ritually impure, the destitute, or those under religious obligation remained unwed past twenty-five.
edits.rationale: Better captures the cultural weight of unmarried status in 1st century Jewish culture

edits.original_text: I'm finishing a roof beam for a widow on the north road. Olive wood. Terrible to work with
edits.revised_text: I'm finishing a roof beam for a widow on the north road. Pine from the northern hills. The grain runs crooked
edits.rationale: Corrects material culture issue - olive wood inappropriate for structural beams

edits.original_text: Hitting back just makes the other side hit harder. The cycle never ends unless someone refuses to swing.
edits.revised_text: Strike a man and he strikes back harder. The grinding never stops unless the wheel breaks.
edits.rationale: Replaces modern phrasing with period-appropriate metaphors

edits.original_text: I've been dreaming of burning cities since I was three years old. Not my cities. My ancestors'.
edits.revised_text: Since I was three, I've been dreaming of cities burning—but they're not my cities. Stone buildings I've never seen, people in ancient clothes running through streets I somehow know. I'm always carrying something—scrolls, books, tools—and I always know exactly where to hide them before the fire reaches us.
edits.rationale: Makes inherited memory more visceral and concrete for reader understanding

edits.revised_text: Forty-second in the line from Tausret—some counted by centuries, not individual lives.
edits.rationale: Addresses mathematical grounding issues with generation counting

edits.original_text: as the last light bled from the sky
edits.revised_text: as daylight faded over the lake
edits.rationale: Replaces overused metaphor with fresher imagery

edits.original_text: I've buried three cousins who got careless. My grandfather died in Alexandria when they burned the library. You think I haven't learned what matters? One brilliant teacher versus eighty families with children? The choice makes itself.
edits.revised_text: Look, centurion—I've buried three cousins who got careless about who they trusted. My grandfather died in Alexandria when they burned the libraries. This network has children, grandparents, people who just want to pass down what their parents taught them. You think I don't care about Jesus? But one teacher against eighty families? The math is ugly, but it's simple.
edits.rationale: Grounds abstract network discussion in Simon's personal experience

edits.original_text: His pulse was in his wrists, which was never a good sign.
edits.revised_text: His pulse quickened—the same way it had before Germania, before Pannonia. But this was a different kind of battlefield, and twenty years had taught him to read the terrain.
edits.rationale: Shows Marcus using veteran instincts rather than just fear

edits.original_text: If there were two of them in Galilee, there might be more. And Rome was already watching.
edits.revised_text: The woman turned, and her eyes met his across the crowd, and Marcus felt the recognition hit like cold water. Not just another carrier—something stronger. As Jesus taught about kingdoms falling, she was seeing exactly when and how this one would. And she was afraid.
edits.rationale: Creates stronger cliffhanger and immediate interpersonal tension
edits.source_note: Series Arc Editor - chapter handoff and Thread Tracker - cliffhanger quality
